title: Playbook - Azure Logic Apps To Microsoft Defender For Endpoint APIs
applies_to: Logic Apps workflows that retrieve Microsoft Defender for Endpoint data for reporting or downstream storage
prerequisites: Defender for Endpoint app registration and permission grants are complete
Logic Apps workflow has a supported authentication path for acquiring a bearer token
Clear target endpoint such as machines, vulnerabilities, recommendations, or score APIs
required_permissions: Defender for Endpoint permissions specific to the endpoint family, such as Machine.Read.All or Score.Read.All
App or delegated access aligned with the intended unattended or user-context workflow
request_sequence: Acquire a token for the Defender for Endpoint API audience expected by the target API family.
Call the target endpoint through the Logic Apps HTTP action, using the canonical base URI or a regional URI when appropriate.
For onboarded-device reporting, query /api/machines and filter by onboardingStatus.
For posture reporting, call score endpoints such as /api/configurationScore when the output needs summarized security posture rather than raw machine rows.
Parse the response JSON and normalize the fields required by downstream storage or reporting.
Persist the normalized result to SharePoint or pass it forward to another reporting pipeline.
implementation_notes: Use Logic Apps for orchestration, but treat Defender for Endpoint auth as a first-class design step because of the legacy audience and permission nuances.
For onboarded-device counts, the simplest pattern is to retrieve filtered machine rows and aggregate them in Logic Apps or downstream in Power BI.
For recurring reports, store extraction time and any business snapshot date so Power BI can distinguish when the metric was observed from when it was reported.
If you combine MDE inventory with Microsoft Graph phishing simulation data in one workflow, keep their authentication and pagination paths separate and only join after normalization.
caveats: A token with the wrong audience can make a correctly permissioned Logic App fail unexpectedly.
Machines is a current-state inventory API, not a historical month-end count API.
Logic Apps knowledge covers the orchestration pattern; exact connector or HTTP auth implementation details still depend on your chosen identity path.
